preFix.title: Making a box shelving unit with a rebated back
preFix.subtitle: Build a freestanding bookcase or display unit with a recessed plywood back panel
preFix.excerpt: How to build a box shelving unit from 18 mm PAR timber or MDF with a rebated back panel, covering cutting the rebate with a router or chisel, gluing and screwing the carcass, fitting fixed and adjustable shelves, and finishing for paint or varnish.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (cabinet-making). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Carpentry for Boys (1914).

content.text: A box shelving unit with a
rebated back

is a step up from basic butt-jointed shelving because the back panel sits recessed into the carcass, adding rigidity and a cleaner appearance. The rebate can be cut with a router, a rebate plane, or simply with a chisel and tenon saw. The result is a freestanding bookcase or display unit suitable for paint or clear varnish finish.
What you will need: materials

18 mm PAR timber or MDF for the sides, top, bottom, and fixed shelves. 9 mm plywood for the back panel. Shelf pins and 5 mm shelf-pin holes drilled in two columns on each side for adjustable shelves. No. 8 x 50 mm screws for the carcass. No. 8 x 25 mm screws for the back panel. PVA wood glue. Timber filler or ready-mixed fine filler for screw heads.
Step 1, cut all parts to size

Mark and cut the two sides, the top, the bottom, and any fixed shelves. The top and bottom fit between the two sides: their length is the internal width of the unit. Cuts must be square: a cut 1 or 2 degrees off square at the end of an 800 mm piece leaves a visible gap at the joint. Check each cut with the try square immediately after sawing and plane or sand back to square if needed.
Step 2, cut the rebate for the back panel

The rebate runs along the back inner edge of both sides, the top, and the bottom. It should be 9 mm wide (to accept the 9 mm plywood back) and 9 mm deep. To cut it by hand: use the tenon saw to make two cuts, one along the face and one across the edge, meeting at the corner of the rebate. Remove the waste with the chisel. Work along the full length, taking light cuts and checking the depth with the combination square. The rebate must be a consistent depth: a deeper rebate at one end means the back panel will not sit flat.
Step 3, drill the shelf-pin holes
Before assembling the carcass, drill the
shelf-pin

holes in the inner face of both sides. Mark two columns of holes at 32 mm spacing (the industry standard), starting 50 mm from the front and back edges. Use a 5 mm brad-point drill bit and a depth stop set to 12 mm. Mark both sides from the same end to ensure the columns are mirrored correctly. Drilling the holes before assembly is much easier than drilling inside a finished carcass.
Step 4, glue and assemble the carcass

Apply PVA to the end grain of the top and bottom pieces and cramp them between the sides. Pre-drill and countersink, then drive two No. 8 x 50 mm screws at each corner through the sides into the top and bottom end grain. Measure the diagonals and adjust the cramps before the glue sets. Any fixed intermediate shelves are glued and screwed in the same way, with two screws per end.
Step 5, fit the back panel

Cut the 9 mm plywood back to size and dry-fit it into the rebate. It should drop in cleanly and bring the carcass to square. Apply a bead of PVA along the rebate and press the back panel in. Nail or screw through the back panel into the rebate at 150 mm centres around the perimeter. Allow the glue to dry fully before removing the cramps.
Variations

A back panel let into a housing groove cut across both sides (rather than into a rebate at the back edge) gives greater rigidity for a deeper unit. For a painted finish, use MDF throughout: it takes paint smoothly without filling. For a clear-varnish finish, use hardwood-veneered ply for the back panel and PAR timber for the sides.
Care

Check that adjustable shelves rest on four pins, two per side. A shelf on three pins sits on two at one end and one at the other and will flex. Do not overload shelves: a heavily loaded shelf can crack the carcass joints over time. The back panel keeps the unit square and must stay securely fixed.

attrs.fix: Shim the short foot with folded card or felt until the unit stands without rocking. Add a felt pad to all four corners to prevent floor scratches.
attrs.cause: One foot is shorter than the others; the carcass is sound but the floor is uneven.
attrs.symptom: Unit racks or twists when placed on an uneven floor

attrs.fix: Add a horizontal rail across the middle of the back panel inside the unit to prevent the bow. A 50 x 18 mm strip glued and screwed across the width at mid-height resolves it.
attrs.cause: The back panel is too thin for the span; 6 mm ply in a wide unit will bow.
attrs.symptom: Back panel bows inward after the unit is loaded

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
